Author: Fahim ul Haq
Title: Your first AI pair-programming session: A 30-minute practice script
Text:
Your first AI pair-programming session: A 30-minute practice script
In my experience teaching thousands of learners at Educative, I’ve worked with individuals switching careers, recent college graduates, or those returning to coding after years in another field. They are eager and motivated, but they are also stepping into a world where AI tools sit right beside them from day one. This is very different from how I learned to code. When I started, debugging meant staring at a terminal until the problem revealed itself. Today, you have a partner sitting in the editor, ready to respond every time you type a message.
That sounds great, but the shift is bigger than it appears. Most new developers are unfamiliar with working with AI coding assistants. They treat it the way many of us treated Stack Overflow in the early days: ask a quick question, hope for the right answer, and copy whatever looks correct into the file. It’s not their fault. They haven’t learned that pair programming, whether with a human or an AI, depends on clarity, structure, and intentionality.
I’ve seen this pattern across different environments. At Facebook, less experienced engineers who progressed the fastest were often the ones who narrated their debugging process during incidents, reading logs line by line, stating what they expected to happen, and calling out mismatches out loud instead of silently guessing. At Microsoft, the teams I worked with took time to break problems into explicit steps before writing code. And at Educative, learners who practice this kind of structured thinking build confidence earlier, even if they haven’t written many programs before.”
Let’s start with the shift in mindset that new developers need to make.
The challenges of AI-assisted coding
New developers often assume the hardest part of using an AI assistant is getting the right prompt. However, the biggest challenge is actually something more fundamental: learning to express your intent clearly. Without that skill, the AI gives you answers that are technically correct but not aligned with what you want. You end up with code that works but teaches you nothing.
At Educative, we’ve studied thousands of early learners going through the same journey. Many start by asking vague questions such as “Help me build an app” or “Fix this code.” They get back a wall of suggestions. They scroll through it, copy something, and hope it works. When it doesn’t, they blame themselves or the tool. But the issue is usually clarity. The AI only sees what you share. It doesn’t understand the rest of the idea in your head.
When I was working on internal developer tooling at Facebook, I quickly learned that the best engineers were not necessarily the ones who wrote code the fastest. They were the ones who explained the problem the cleanest. A design review often revealed gaps we didn’t see until we tried to articulate the idea. The same is true for AI pair programming. The clearer you are, the better the assistant becomes.
Another issue beginners face is pacing. Many rush ahead and ask the AI to generate an entire program in one shot. A common failure mode looks like this: the code runs, maybe even passes a quick test, but the learner can’t explain what half the functions do or where to start when something breaks.
The improvement usually comes when they adopt a simple habit, working in small steps. When learners slow down and implement one function at a time, they start forming a mental map of the program. That’s when things change. They’re able to predict what will break, make small edits with confidence, and debug without panic. The code stops feeling like magic and starts feeling manageable.
Finally, new developers often feel pressure to sound technical. This leads to lengthy and complicated prompts that fail to convey their intended meaning. In real-world engineering teams, no one expects you to speak in perfect technical language. They expect honesty and clarity. When you ask another engineer for help, you don’t perform; you explain. That is the mindset you want to bring to the assistant as well.
With these challenges in mind, let’s move into the 30-minute script.
A 30-minute script for your first AI pair-programming session
This script is not designed to showcase cleverness. It’s meant to reinforce the habits used on production engineering teams. You can work through it using any coding assistant or LLM-based tool. The goal is to build reliable problem-solving habits rather than optimize for speed.
Minute 0–5: Set the stage with intent
Begin your session by stating your goal in clear, straightforward language. It doesn’t need to sound technical. You can write something like this:
I’m learning how to use an AI coding assistant. I want to build a small CLI to-do app in Python to practice. I want to work in a step-by-step manner. Please pause after each step so I can check my understanding.
This is basic but important. You’re giving the assistant constraints, and you are also permitting yourself to move at a sustainable pace. This mirrors how we wrote internal design docs at Microsoft. We didn’t start with the final answer. We began by outlining what we were trying to accomplish.
If the assistant provides a long answer, you can add:
Keep responses concise and focused so I can follow along.
You’re shaping the working environment the same way you would with a human partner.
Minutes 5–10: Agree on the structure before coding
Ask the assistant:
Before writing code, propose a simple plan for the program and explain why this plan makes sense for a beginner.
New developers often overlook this step, but it is crucial to their success. Understanding structure is more important than any single function. When I worked with junior engineers at Facebook, this was the moment when we identified misunderstandings early. A simple plan is easier to adjust than 150 lines of code.
A typical plan might include:
• A function to show a menu
• A function to add a task
• A function to list tasks
• A loop to run the app
This structure works well for beginners because each piece has a single responsibility. The menu handles user input, the task functions manage state, and the loop controls program flow. Nothing is hidden, and nothing does too much at once. When something breaks, you know where to look.
It may seem simple, but this separation mirrors how larger systems are built, just at a scale that’s easy to reason about. It’s one of the first habits you want to build: agree on the outline before touching the keyboard.
If something in the plan feels confusing, ask about it. Think of it as a low-stakes design review. This is where learning happens.
Minutes 10–20: Build the program in small pieces
Now ask the assistant:
Let’s implement the first part of the plan. Start with a small function and explain the choices you’re making.
Keep the pieces small. This teaches you how to read code with intention. It also keeps the assistant aligned with your pace.
If the assistant gives you too much code, say:
Please break this into smaller steps so I can learn as we go.
This reflects how real engineering work typically occurs: building one small piece, verifying that it behaves as expected, and only then adding the next layer. When I joined Educative, we followed this same approach with new content, reviewing each piece in isolation before expanding it.
As you code, ask questions:
• Why use a list here?
• What are other ways to store tasks?
• Can you show a simpler version first?
These aren’t technical tricks. There are ways of training your engineering mind to stay active rather than drifting into autopilot.
Minutes 20–25: Test each function
At this point, you should have something that runs. It doesn’t need to be perfect. Now do what real teams do next: verify it with a few simple test cases, especially edge cases.
Ask the AI assistant:
Propose 5–7 small test cases for this CLI to-do app, including edge cases. For each one, tell me the expected behavior.
Examples of edge cases to include:
You try to list tasks when there are none.
You enter an invalid menu choice (letters, empty input, or a number outside the options).
You try to delete a task number that doesn’t exist.
You add an empty task (just pressing “Enter”).
You add a task with leading/trailing spaces.
Then say:
Let’s run these test cases one by one. After each test, help me compare what happened with what we expected. If something fails, explain why it failed and suggest a small fix, but don’t rewrite the whole program.
This is how you stay in control with AI. You’re not just generating code, you’re validating behavior. And when something breaks, you’ll have a clear reproduction step, which makes debugging calmer and faster.
Minutes 25–30: Reflect
End the session with reflection. Ask:
Summarize what I practiced today. What skills did I use? What should I try in my next session?
Reflection is one of the most underrated habits in early engineering careers. When we developed our early courses at Educative, we realized that learners who took the time to reflect on their work developed confidence much faster. They weren’t guessing about their progress; they could see it.
Your reflection might reveal:
• You learned to express intent.
• You practiced reviewing structure before coding.
• You broke tasks into small steps.
• You debugged with intention.
• You asked questions to improve clarity.
Those are real engineering skills. They create momentum, and momentum creates confidence.
Conclusion
New developers often feel unsure when they open their editors, but that feeling is normal and shared by engineers at every level. A simple 30-minute practice session helps you slow down, think clearly, and build steady habits around structure, pacing, and reflection. AI tools can feel fast and overwhelming, but working in small steps keeps you in control and strengthens your confidence. If you’re starting your journey, focus on momentum, not mastery. Each session builds upon the foundation laid in the previous one. And if you want guided practice, Educative offers hands-on paths that support you as you grow at your own pace.
